{{Short description|none}}

[[File:Zimbabwe national football team, 2009.jpg|thumb|2009 Image of Zimbabwe national football team]]
The '''Zimbabwe national football team''' ({{langx|sn|Chikwata chenhabvu chenyika yeZimbabwe}}), nicknamed ''The Warriors'', represents [[Zimbabwe]] in men's international [[Association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Zimbabwe Football Association]] (ZIFA), formerly known as the Football Association of Rhodesia. The team has never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] finals, but has qualified for the [[Africa Cup of Nations]] six times. Zimbabwe has also won the [[COSAFA Cup]] six times. The team represents both [[FIFA]] and the [[Confederation of African Football]] (CAF).

==History==
Zimbabwe played their first official match against the [[England Amateur national football team]] as part of the latter's tour of South Africa and Rhodesia in June 1929. Zimbabwe lost their first two matches against England 4–0 and 6–1, respectively.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.englandfootballonline.com/MatchRsl/MatchRslUnoffpg1.html |title=England Matches&nbsp;– Unofficial |publisher=Englandfootballonline.com |access-date=2016-09-06 |archive-date=11 May 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180511012709/http://www.englandfootballonline.com/MatchRsl/MatchRslUnoffpg1.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In 1965, following Southern [[Rhodesia's Unilateral Declaration of Independence]] as Rhodesia, FIFA requested that the Football Association of Rhodesia reform to be a multi-racial organisation. Prior to this, only [[white Rhodesians]] were selected for the national football team but after 1965 the team became multi-racial.<ref>{{cite book | first =Paul | last =Gilchrist  | title =The Politics of Sport: Community, Mobility, Identity | publisher = Routledge | year = 2013 | isbn = 978-1317990994 | page = 51}}</ref> In 1969, Rhodesia took part in the Oceanic [[1970 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)|1970 FIFA World Cup qualification]] tournament. This was their first attempt to qualify for the FIFA World Cup. Contrary to the team being viewed as the representative team of white Rhodesians, the team was multi-racial including black players.<ref name=fifa /> They were drawn against the [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia national football team]]. Both legs were held in [[Lourenço Marques]], [[Portuguese Mozambique]] as the Rhodesian team were unable to get Australian visas. Rhodesia drew the first leg 1–1 but lost the second leg 3–1 thus eliminating Rhodesia from qualification.<ref name=fifa>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/news/y=2016/m=9/news=when-rhodesia-flirted-with-the-world-cup-2830489.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160904160842/http://www.fifa.com/news/y=2016/m=9/news=when-rhodesia-flirted-with-the-world-cup-2830489.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=4 September 2016 |title=When Rhodesia flirted with the World Cup |publisher=FIFA.com |date=2016-08-29 |access-date=2016-09-06}}</ref>

In 1980, following the country's reconstitution as Zimbabwe, they played their first FIFA World Cup qualifying match for 11 years against the [[Cameroon national football team]]. However they lost 2–1 on aggregate after a 1–0 win in the first leg in [[Harare|Salisbury]] and a 2–0 loss in the second leg.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.11v11.com/teams/cameroon/tab/opposingTeams/opposition/Zimbabwe/|title=Cameroon national football team: record v Zimbabwe|website=11v11.com|publisher=AFS Enterprises Limited|access-date=6 September 2016|archive-date=17 September 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160917193222/http://www.11v11.com/teams/cameroon/tab/opposingTeams/opposition/Zimbabwe/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/matches/round=7991/match=838/index.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160917223644/http://www.fifa.com/worldcup/matches/round=7991/match=838/index.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=17 September 2016 |title=1982 FIFA World Cup Spain&nbsp;– Matches&nbsp;– Zimbabwe-Cameroon |publisher=FIFA.com |date=2016-07-23 |access-date=2016-09-06}}</ref> Following this, the country passed a law that people who held [[British passport]]s would not be permitted to hold a [[Zimbabwean passport]], which mean that players such as goalkeeper [[Bruce Grobbelaar]], who is considered to be Zimbabwe's greatest goalkeeper,<ref>{{cite web |url=https://boxscorenews.com/bruce-grobbelaar-zimbabwes-legendary-goalkeeper-p167033-272.htm |title=Bruce Grobbelaar: Zimbabwe's Legendary Goalkeeper |publisher=Boxscore World Sportswire |date=2022-08-28 |access-date=2023-04-01 |archive-date=1 April 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230401152750/https://boxscorenews.com/bruce-grobbelaar-zimbabwes-legendary-goalkeeper-p167033-272.htm#:~:text=Bruce%20Grobbelaar%20was%20one%20of,league%20cups%2C%20amongst%20other%20trophies. |url-status=live }}</ref>were not selected for the national team for 10 years.<ref name="guard">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2015/aug/19/peter-ndlovu-bruce-grobbelaar-zimbabwe|title=When Peter Ndlovu and Bruce Grobbelaar made Zimbabwe dare to dream.|last=Hawkey|first=Ian|work=The Guardian|date=19 August 2015 |access-date=2015-08-20|archive-date=2 October 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181002154022/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2015/aug/19/peter-ndlovu-bruce-grobbelaar-zimbabwe|url-status=live}}</ref> Following a change in policy that allowed Grobbelaar to play for Zimbabwe, who entered the country on his British passport, Zimbabwe under manager [[Reinhard Fabisch]] were one match away from qualifying for the [[1994 FIFA World Cup]]. However, they lost their final qualifying match to Cameroon.<ref name=guard /><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/94q.html |title=World Cup 1994 Qualifying |publisher=Rsssf |date=2016-06-09 |access-date=2016-09-06 |archive-date=31 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230331190633/https://rsssf.org/tables/94q.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

In 2004, Zimbabwe qualified for their first [[Africa Cup of Nations]]. During their first match against Egypt, their former anthem "[[Ishe Komborera Africa]]" was accidentally played instead of "[[Simudzai Mureza wedu weZimbabwe]]", an act which Information Minister [[Jonathan Moyo]] called "a cheap attempt by the organisers to demoralise our boys".<ref>{{cite web |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/africa/3429937.stm |title=Anger over Zimbabwe anthem gaffe |publisher=BBC News |date=2004-01-26 |access-date=2016-09-06 |archive-date=29 February 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040229173808/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/africa/3429937.stm |url-status=live }}</ref>

In 2015, the Zimbabwe national football team were banned from participating in [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualifying]] due to an unpaid debt to former coach, [[José Claudinei]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/31851586 |title=Zimbabwe expelled from 2018 World Cup |publisher=BBC Sport |date=2015-03-12 |access-date=2016-09-06 |archive-date=30 June 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180630063602/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/31851586 |url-status=live }}</ref> At the time, the team was experiencing its strongest period for many years, qualifying for both the [[2017 Africa Cup of Nations|2017]] and [[2019 Africa Cup of Nations]].

On 1 March 2022, Zimbabwe, along with [[Kenya national football team|Kenya]], was suspended again from international sport due to the interference of the government. Earlier in November 2021, [[Harare]] and [[Nairobi]] dissolved their federations and were replaced with government-officials. On 31 March, the suspension was made indefinitely and was ratified by FIFA. Suspension is set until Zimbabwe and Kenya meet the demands given by FIFA. The team has produced some of the finest footballers the likes of the legendary Peter Ndlovu who played for Zimbabwe 100 times. He featured in the English premier for Coventry City, Birmingham City, Sheffield United and Huddersfield. Peter Ndlovu is well remembered for the hatrick he scored at Anfield against Liverpool, Bruce Grobelaar former Liverpool Goalkeeper, Norman Mapeza former Galatasary defender, Benjan Mwaruwaru former Man city player. Knowledge Musona former Anderletch and Bundesliga player. Khama Billiat former Mamelodi Sundowns and Kaizer Chiefs player.

{{footballbox collapsible
|date = 26 May
|time = {{UTZ|19:30|+1}}
|round = [[2026 Unity Cup]]
|score = 2–0
|report = https://www.espn.co.uk/football/match/_/gameId/401867936

==Coaching history==
[[File:Bruce Grobbelaar 2009.jpg|thumb|100px|[[Bruce Grobbelaar]] played for Zimbabwe and managed Zimbabwe]]
:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{Div col|colwidth=22em}}
* {{flagicon|SCO}} [[Danny McLennan]] (1965–1969)
* {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Bill Asprey]] (1975–1977)
* {{flagicon|SCO}} [[John Rugg (football manager)|John Rugg]] (1980–1981)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Shepherd Murape]] (1981–1983)
* {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Mick Poole (footballer)|Mick Poole]] (1985)
* {{flagicon|GHA}} [[Ben Koufie]] (1988–1992)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Reinhard Fabisch]] (1992–1995)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Rudi Gutendorf]] (1995–1996)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Bruce Grobbelaar]]'' (1996)
* {{flagicon|SCO}} [[Ian Porterfield]] (1996–1997)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Sunday Chidzambwa]]'' (1997)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Bruce Grobbelaar]]'' (1997)
* {{flagicon|POR}} [[Roy Barreto]] (1997–1998)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Bruce Grobbelaar]]'' (1998)
* {{flagicon|NED}} [[Clemens Westerhof]] (1998–2000)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Misheck Chidzambwa]] (2000)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Sunday Chidzambwa]] (2000–2002)
* {{flagicon|POL}} [[Wiesław Grabowski]] (2002)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Sunday Chidzambwa]] (2003–2004)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Rahman Gumbo]] (2004)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Charles Mhlauri]] (2004–2007)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Sunday Chidzambwa]]'' (2007)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Norman Mapeza]]'' (2007)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Luke Masomore]]'' (2007–2008)
* {{flagicon|BRA}} [[José Claudinei]] (2008)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Sunday Chidzambwa]] (2008–2009)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Norman Mapeza]] (2009–2010)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Tom Saintfiet]] (2010)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Madinda Ndlovu]] (2010–2011)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Norman Mapeza]] (2011–2012)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Rahman Gumbo]]'' (2012)
* {{flagicon|GER}} ''[[Klaus Dieter Pagels]]'' (2012–2013)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Ian Gorowa]]'' (2013–2014)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Callisto Pasuwa]] (2015–2017)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Wilson Mutekede]]'' (2017)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Sunday Chidzambwa]] (2017–2019)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Joey Antipas]]'' (2019–2020)
* {{flagicon|CRO}} [[Zdravko Logarušić]] (2020–2021)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Norman Mapeza]]'' (2021–2022)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Wilson Mutekede]]'' (2022)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Shepherd Murape]] (2022–2023)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} [[Sunday Chidzambwa]] (2023)
* {{flagicon|BRA}} ''[[Baltemar Brito]]'' (2023–2024)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Norman Mapeza]]'' (2024)
* {{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Jairos Tapera]]'' (2024)
* {{Flagicon|GER}} [[Michael Nees]] (2024–2025)
* {{Flagicon|ROU}} [[Mario Marinică]] (2025–2026)
* {{Flagicon|ZIM}} [[Kaitano Tembo]] (2026–)<ref>{{Cite web |last=Madyira |first=Michael |date=April 27, 2026 |title=Kaitano Tembo appointed Zimbabwe coach as Marinica resigns |url=https://farpost.co.za/2026/04/27/kaitano-tembo-appointed-zimbabwe-coach-as-marinica-resigns/ |access-date=2026-04-27 |website=FARPost ZA |language=en-US}}</ref>
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following players were selected for the [[2026 Unity Cup]] matches against [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]] and either [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] or [[India national football team|India]] on 26 and 30 May 2026, respectively.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/footballzim/posts/pfbid022NjH6ifuJKQLUkmWFyb8amgDEPWYbFDWp4kHD9uVEJHNCqcYZFDGVNxYLSxH3SMxl|title=Warriors Squad for Unity Cup in the United Kingdom|publisher=[[Zimbabwe Football Association]]|date=11 May 2026|access-date=21 May 2026|via=[[Facebook]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/footballzim/posts/pfbid02t99oBzgCGPT6d5S6PkzQyg2RkaWoWmZiNVYuTCCCapofHsZgaP8pmBh7Dey7ps1wl|title=Warriors Squad Update|publisher=Zimbabwe Football Association|date=20 May 2026|access-date=21 May 2026|via=[[Facebook]]}}</ref>

''Caps and goals are correct as of 26 May 2026, after the match against [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]].''

{{nat fs g start}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up for Zimbabwe in the last 12 months.

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=Tymon Machope|age={{birth date and age|1993|07|03|df=yes}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=TelOne|clubnat=ZIM|latest=v. {{fb|QAT}}, 16 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Khama Billiat]]|age={{birth date and age|1990|08|19|df=yes}}|caps=61|goals=18|club=[[Scottland F.C.|Scottland]]|clubnat=ZIM|latest=v. {{fb|LES}}, 13 October 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Terrence Dzvukamanja]]|age={{bda|1994|5|5|df=y}}|caps=26|goals=1|club=[[Scottland F.C.|Scottland]]|clubnat=ZIM|latest=v. {{fb|LES}}, 13 October 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=Tawanda Macheke|age={{birth date and age|1996|08|12|df=yes}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=TelOne|clubnat=ZIM|latest=v. {{fb|NIG}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>DEC</sup> Player refused to join the team after the call-up.<br/>
<sup>INJ</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to an injury.<br/>
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad.<br/>
<sup>RET</sup> Player has retired from international football.<br/>
<sup>SUS</sup> Suspended from the national team, red or yellow cards.
{{nat fs end}}

==Records==
{{Updated|29 December 2025}}<ref>{{cite web |title=Zimbabwe |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/208/Zimbabwe.html |website=National Football Teams}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Zimbabwe - Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/zimb-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF}}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Zimbabwe.''

==Competitive record==
===FIFA World Cup===
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
!colspan=9|[[FIFA World Cup]] record
!rowspan="39" |

===COSAFA Cup===
{{colbegin}}
*[[1997 COSAFA Cup|1997]]&nbsp;– Qualifying round
*[[1998 COSAFA Cup|1998]]&nbsp;– Second place
*[[1999 COSAFA Cup|1999]]&nbsp;– Quarter-finals
*[[2000 COSAFA Cup|2000]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[2001 COSAFA Cup|2001]]&nbsp;– Second place
*[[2002 COSAFA Cup|2002]]&nbsp;– Quarter-finals
*[[2003 COSAFA Cup|2003]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[2004 COSAFA Cup|2004]]&nbsp;– Semi-finals
*[[2005 COSAFA Cup|2005]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[2006 COSAFA Cup|2006]]&nbsp;– Semi-finals
*[[2007 COSAFA Cup|2007]]&nbsp;– First round
*[[2008 COSAFA Cup|2008]]&nbsp;– Quarter-finals
*[[2009 COSAFA Cup|2009]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*2010&nbsp;– ''Cancelled''
*[[2013 COSAFA Cup|2013]]&nbsp;– Second place
*[[2015 COSAFA Cup|2015]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[2016 COSAFA Cup|2016]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[2017 COSAFA Cup|2017]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[2018 COSAFA Cup|2018]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[2019 COSAFA Cup|2019]]&nbsp;– '''Third place'''
*[[2020 COSAFA Cup|2020]]&nbsp;– ''Cancelled''
*[[2021 COSAFA Cup|2021]]&nbsp;– Group stage
{{colend}}

===CECAFA Cup===
{{colbegin}}
*[[1981 CECAFA Cup|1981]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[1982 CECAFA Cup|1982]]&nbsp;– Third place
*[[1983 CECAFA Cup|1983]]&nbsp;– Second place
*[[1984 CECAFA Cup|1984]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[1985 CECAFA Cup|1985]]&nbsp;– '''Winners'''
*[[1987 CECAFA Cup|1987]]&nbsp;– Second place
*[[1988 CECAFA Cup|1988]]&nbsp;– Fourth place
*[[1989 CECAFA Cup|1989]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[1990 CECAFA Cup|1990]]&nbsp;– Group stage
*[[2009 CECAFA Cup|2009]]&nbsp;– Quarter-finals
*[[2011 CECAFA Cup|2011]]&nbsp;– Quarter-finals
{{colend}}

==Honours==
===Intercontinental===
* '''[[Afro-Asian Games]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze Medal (1): [[Football at the 2003 Afro-Asian Games|2003]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asafrgam01.html|title=Afro-Asian Games 2003|access-date=20 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220928130141/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asafrgam01.html|archive-date=28 September 2022|website=[[RSSSF]]|first1=Arunava|last1=Chaudhuri|first2=Karel|last2=Stokkermans|date=2001}}</ref>

===Regional===
* '''[[COSAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (6):''' [[2000 COSAFA Cup|2000]], [[2003 COSAFA Cup|2003]], [[2005 COSAFA Cup|2005]], [[2009 COSAFA Cup|2009]], [[2017 COSAFA Cup|2017]], [[2018 COSAFA Cup|2018]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (3): [[1998 COSAFA Cup|1998]], [[2001 COSAFA Cup|2001]], [[2013 COSAFA Cup|2013]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2019 COSAFA Cup|2019]]
* '''[[CECAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1):''' [[1985 CECAFA Cup|1985]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1983 CECAFA Cup|1983]], [[1987 CECAFA Cup|1987]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[1982 CECAFA Cup|1982]]
